summary: Drew DeVault reviews the PinePhone developer edition, expressing intense excitement about a phone with hardware compatible with upstream Linux drivers and zero blobs. He acknowledges the software is nowhere near ready for daily use — phone calls, SMS, and LTE all require hacks or don't fully work — but argues the right people from the postmarketOS, KDE, and kernel hacking communities are actively building the necessary software. He describes his own work on a mobile Wayland compositor based on Sway and various shell components. The review concludes that this is the phone the free software community has been waiting for: affordable hardware that the community can actually build a fully open mobile OS around.
key_points: The PinePhone hardware works with upstream Linux drivers and zero blobs, which is unprecedented for a phone
Software support is extremely early — phone calls, SMS, LTE, and MMS range from hacky to non-functional, making it suitable only for developers right now
DeVault is building mobile Wayland compositor components based on Sway/wlroots, including an app drawer, dialer, on-screen keyboard, and notification system
The phone costs $150, a fraction of typical smartphone prices, making it accessible for experimentation
The free software community has rallied around the PinePhone with unprecedented support from pmOS, KDE, and kernel developers
money_quotes: Holy shit! This is the phone I have always wanted.
Right now, there are very few people, perhaps only dozens, for whom this phone is the right phone, given the current level of software support.
However, it seems to me that all of the right people are working on the software end of this phone.
Like any other computer I've purchased, I planned on immediately flashing my own OS on it.
That these features can be done in an annoying way means that it's feasible to write applications that do this in a convenient way.
I have been waiting for this phone for years and years and years.
I knew that the free software community was totally capable of building the software for such a phone, if only the hardware existed.
And it's only $150! I could buy four of them for the price of the typical smartphone! And I just might!
key_insight: The PinePhone represents the hardware breakthrough the free software community needed — affordable, blob-free, and Linux-compatible — and the community is already rising to build the software stack it requires.
